summary: codebuff-cli typosquats CodebuffAI and drops an opaque binary from an impersonator's GitHub releases
details: The package name and README impersonate the legitimate CodebuffAI `codebuff` project (codebuff.com), but the `bin/codebuff.cjs` shim downloads its executable from `https://github.com/Marcus-Mok-GH/codebuff-cli/releases/...` — a personal GitHub account unrelated to the CodebuffAI publisher. The CLI is the package's documented entry point (`npx codebuff-cli` / running `codebuff` per the copied README), so on first invocation `bin/codebuff.cjs` calls `https://api.github.com/repos/Marcus-Mok-GH/codebuff-cli/releases/latest`, fetches the platform binary from the resulting `releases/download/<latest-tag>` URL with no signature or hash verification, writes it to disk, and executes it via a child process with `stdio: 'inherit'`. The cache key is the remote `latest` tag rather than the installed npm version, so any new release the impersonator publishes is automatically pulled and run on every existing installation — a live channel for rotating payloads. The spawned binary is launched with `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` in its environment, disabling TLS certificate validation for all HTTPS traffic it makes (including to the README-documented backend `https://fireworks-api-backend.vercel.app`, an anonymous Vercel deployment that is not codebuff.com). Net effect on a user who runs `codebuff` thinking they have the legitimate tool: arbitrary attacker-controlled bytes execute on their machine, prompts/code/agent traffic flow to attacker infrastructure, and outbound TLS is unverified.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
